title: Priority Queue
description: Putting the heap structure to work: finding the Kth largest element in O(n log k) using a min-heap of size k.
objectives: Solve the Kth Largest Element problem using a min-heap
Understand why a min-heap (not max-heap) of size k is the right tool
Trace the algorithm on a real example
Understand the O(n log k) complexity

sections.text: 1. Recap: The Structure We Built, Now Put to Work
Heap built the actual sift-up mechanics by hand. priority_queue, covered much earlier, gives us that exact structure ready-made. Let's use it to solve a genuinely useful problem.
Sorting the entire array first works, but costs O(n log n) — recall Sorting using sort(). For large n and small k, there's a better way.
3. The Trick: A Min-Heap of Size k

Keep a min-heap holding only the k largest values seen so far. Once it has k elements, compare each new number against the heap's smallest (its top) — if the new number is bigger, the current smallest can't possibly be among the k largest anymore, so it's popped and replaced. After processing everything, the heap's top is exactly the Kth largest value.

sections.title: Tracing it (k=2)
points: push 1 → heap: {1,2,3}, size 3 > k → pop smallest (1) → heap: {2,3}
push 5 → heap: {2,3,5}, size 3 > k → pop smallest (2) → heap: {3,5}
push 6 → heap: {3,5,6}, size 3 > k → pop smallest (3) → heap: {5,6}
push 4 → heap: {4,5,6}, size 3 > k → pop smallest (4) → heap: {5,6}
Final heap top (smallest of the remaining two) = 5 — the 2nd largest overall

sections.text: 5. Why a Min-Heap, Not a Max-Heap

This is a common point of confusion: we want the LARGEST k values overall, but we track them using a MIN-heap — because the value we need to check and possibly discard is always the smallest of the k values we're currently keeping. A max-heap would put the wrong element (the current largest) at the top, which isn't what needs comparing against new arrivals.

The heap never holds more than k elements, so every push/pop costs O(log k) (recall Heap's O(log n) reasoning, applied here with a heap capped at size k), and this happens for each of the n input elements — O(n log k) overall. When k is much smaller than n, this beats sorting the whole array in O(n log n).

sections.title: A talent show keeping only its top k finalists
sections.text: A talent show with a strict cutoff of k finalists doesn't need to rank every contestant who ever auditioned — it only needs to track the current weakest finalist, so a stronger new contestant knows exactly who to bump. That's the min-heap of size k: only the boundary case (the weakest of the current best) ever needs comparing.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using a max-heap instead of a min-heap for this problem — the comparison needed is against the CURRENT SMALLEST of the top-k group, which only a min-heap exposes at the top.
Forgetting to cap the heap at size k — without popping when size exceeds k, this just becomes an expensive way to sort everything.
Sorting the whole array when k is small and n is large — O(n log k) is meaningfully better than O(n log n) in that case.
Forgetting the priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> syntax from Custom Comparators — plain priority_queue<int> defaults to a max-heap, the wrong tool here.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How would you find the Kth largest element in an array efficiently?
questions.answer: Maintain a min-heap of size k; push each element, and if the heap exceeds size k, pop the smallest. After processing all elements, the heap's top is the Kth largest.

questions.question: Why is a min-heap used to find the largest elements, rather than a max-heap?
questions.answer: Because the value that needs comparing and possibly discarding is always the current smallest among the k largest values kept so far — only a min-heap exposes that value at the top.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the min-heap approach to Kth Largest Element, and when does it beat sorting?
questions.answer: O(n log k), since the heap never exceeds size k; this beats sorting the whole array (O(n log n)) whenever k is significantly smaller than n.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Finding the Kth largest element efficiently uses a min-heap capped at size k: push each number, and whenever the heap exceeds k elements, pop the smallest — after processing the whole array, the heap's top is exactly the Kth largest. A min-heap, not a max-heap, is used because the value that needs checking against each new number is always the current smallest of the top-k group, which only a min-heap keeps at the top. Since the heap never holds more than k elements, this runs in O(n log k), beating a full O(n log n) sort whenever k is much smaller than n.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Priority Queue (Kth Largest)
sections.text: Kth largest = a talent show keeping only k finalists. Min-heap tracks the CURRENT WEAKEST finalist, so a stronger newcomer knows who to bump. Top of the min-heap = the answer.

sections.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an unsorted array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element (the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct one).
hints: This is this lesson's exact worked example — the min-heap-of-size-k technique.
Keep a min-heap capped at size k; whenever it exceeds k, pop the smallest.
After processing every number, the heap's top is the kth largest.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort the whole array and index directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's min-heap of size k.

sections.title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
sections.problemStatement: Design KthLargest, initialized with k and an initial array of numbers, supporting add(val) which adds a new number and returns the current kth largest.
hints: This is Kth Largest Element in an Array, but PERSISTENT across many add() calls instead of computed once — the min-heap needs to be kept alive as a member variable between calls.
Initialize the heap with the starting numbers using this lesson's cap-at-size-k technique.
Each add() just does one more push (and a pop if the heap now exceeds k) — the heap's top is always the current answer, no need to recompute from scratch.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all numbers in a vector; sort on every add() call to find the kth largest.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A persistent min-heap capped at size k — each add() is a single O(log k) push (plus a possible pop).
